papers.title: On the origins of extreme velocity stars as revealed by large-scale Galactic surveys
papers.author_names: Qing-Zheng Li Yang Huang Xiao-Bo Dong Hua-Wei Zhang Timothy C. Beers Zhen Yuan

abstract.text: We assemble a large sample of 12,784 high-velocity stars with total velocity $\\\\it{V}_{\\\\rm{GSR}}\\\\ge {\\\\rm 300}$ km s$^{-1}$, selected from RAVE DR5, SDSS DR12, LAMOST DR8, APOGEE DR16, GALAH DR2, and $Gaia$ EDR3. In this sample, 52 are marginally hypervelocity star (HVS) candidates that have $\\\\it{V}_{\\\\rm{GSR}}$ exceeding their local escape velocities within $2\\\\sigma$ confidence levels, 40 of which are discovered for the first time. All candidates are metal-poor, late-type halo stars, significantly different from the previous identified HVSs, which are largely massive early-type stars, discovered by extreme radial velocity. This finding suggests that our newly identified HVS candidates are ejected by different mechanisms from the previous population. To investigate their origins, for 547 extreme velocity stars with ${V}_{\\\\rm{GSR}}\\\\ge0.8V_{\\\\rm{esc}}$, we reconstruct their backward-integrated trajectories in the Galactic potential. According to the orbital analysis, no candidates are found to be definitely ejected from the Galactic-center (GC), while 8 metal-poor extreme velocity stars are found to have a closest distance to the GC within 1kpc. Intriguingly, 15 extreme velocity stars (including 2 HVS candidates) are found to have experienced close encounters with the Sagittarius dSph, suggesting that they originated from this dSph. This hypothesis is supported by an analysis of the [$\\\\alpha$/Fe]--[Fe/H] diagram. From a preliminary analysis of all the 547 extreme velocity stars, we propose a general picture: Star ejection from Galactic subsystems such as dwarf galaxies and globular clusters can be an important channel to produce extreme velocity stars or even HVSs, particularly the metal-poor late-type halo population.

triage.label: rule-direct
triage.reason: Direct title match: high-velocity or extreme-velocity stars

brief.tldr: [astronomical research paper] A large-scale survey of 12,784 high-velocity stars reveals 52 metal-poor, late-type halo candidates with velocities exceeding local escape speeds (marginally HVSs), 40 of which are newly discovered. Orbital backward integration shows no ejection from the Galactic center, but 8 stars come within 1 kpc of it, and 15 exhibit close encounters with the Sagittarius dSph—supported by [α/Fe]-[Fe/H] chemical abundance patterns. The results suggest that ejection from dwarf galaxies or globular clusters via dynamical interactions, rather than black hole binaries or supernovae, is a key origin channel for this population of extreme velocity stars.

source_fields.title: On the origins of extreme velocity stars as revealed by large-scale Galactic surveys

source_fields.sections_text: Data: This section describes the use of data from multiple large-scale Galactic spectroscopic surveys—RAVE, SDSS, LAMOST, APOGEE, and GALAH—along with Gaia astrometry, to obtain stellar parameters and kinematic information with well-characterized uncertainties. Summary: This study identifies 52 new late-type, metal-poor HiVel stars, including 40 previously unreported HVS candidates, and finds evidence that their extreme velocities likely arise from ejection events in Galactic subsystems like dwarf galaxies or globular clusters. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel framework that combines high-precision kinematic data from Gaia and large-scale spectroscopic surveys to rigorously test and distinguish between black hole ejection and supernova kick mechanisms for hypervelocity stars, addressing the long-standing problem of identifying the dominant origin of such extreme stellar velocities in the Milky Way. Comparisons With Other Work: This section compares the authors' HiVel sample with previous studies, showing that earlier works overestimate distances and velocities due to incorrect Gaia parallax zero-point corrections and stricter selection criteria exclude many potential HVS candidates. Selection Of High-Velocity Stars: This section details the coordinate systems, distance estimation via a Bayesian approach using Gaia parallaxes and stellar density priors, and the derivation of 3D velocities for a large sample of stars from multiple spectroscopic surveys. Probable Origins Of Extreme Velocity Stars: The probable origins of extreme velocity stars—particularly late-type, metal-poor halo stars—differ from previously known early-type stars, with evidence suggesting they may be bound halo stars or products of distinct ejection mechanisms, including potential interactions with the Galactic center, as explored through backward orbital integrations in a modeled Galactic potential.

title: On the origins of extreme velocity stars as revealed by large-scale Galactic surveys

catalog_assessment.evidence: Abstract explicitly states assembly of a sample of 12,784 high-velocity stars from multiple surveys (RAVE, SDSS, LAMOST, APOGEE, GALAH, Gaia), identifies 52 HVS candidates with 40 newly discovered, and provides orbital analysis and chemical abundance patterns.
